perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS ADS and the Second Party (the “Parties”) have an interest in participating in discussions wherein either Party might share information with the other that the disclosing Party considers to be proprietary and confidential to itself (“Confidential Information”);
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS ADS and the Second Party (the “Parties”) have an interest in participating in discussions wherein either Party might share information with the other that the disclosing Party considers to be proprietary, but not necessarily confidential to itself (“Confidential Information”);
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of 'Confidential Information' to mean proprietary but not necessarily confidential, the agreement introduces ambiguity. The original text defines it as proprietary *and* confidential, creating a stronger level of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which many states have adopted, defines a trade secret as information that (a) derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and (b) is subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. This modified definition undermines the secrecy requirement, potentially removing information from trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the definition of 'Confidential Information' from 'proprietary and confidential' to 'proprietary, but not necessarily confidential'. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the secrecy requirement of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 2 says that the UTSA defines trade secret misappropriation and aims to ensure consistent trade secret law across states. By weakening the definition of Confidential Information, the contract potentially removes information from trade secret protection, contradicting the UTSA's purpose of protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: When informed of the proprietary and confidential nature of Confidential Information that has been disclosed by the other Party, the receiving Party (“Recipient”) shall, for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure, refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to any contractor or other third party without prior, written approval from the disclosing Party and shall protect such Confidential Information from inadvertent disclosure to a third party using the same care and diligence that the Recipient uses to protect its own proprietary and confidential information, but in no case less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: When informed of the proprietary and confidential nature of Confidential Information that has been disclosed by the other Party, the receiving Party (“Recipient”) shall, for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure, refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to any contractor or other third party without prior, written approval from the disclosing Party and shall protect such Confidential Information from inadvertent disclosure to a third party using minimal care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'three (3) years' to 'one (1) year' and 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' reduces the protection period and the standard of care, conflicting with common trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 is a widely recognized articulation of trade secret law. Comment B to § 757 discusses factors determining appropriate protection including the extent of measures taken to guard the secrecy of information and the period it should be guarded. Reducing the protection to 'minimal care' and decreasing the time of protection contradicts the standard of conduct that is expected.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'three (3) years' to 'one (1) year' and 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' directly contradicts the standard of care expected in trade secret protection. The LLM's law explanation references the Restatement (First) of Torts § 757, which outlines factors determining appropriate trade secret protection, including the extent of measures and duration. By reducing both the protection period and the standard of care, the modified contract fails to provide the level of protection expected under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party may suffer injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to attempt to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irreparable injury' to 'may suffer injury,' 'shall be entitled to obtain' to 'shall be entitled to attempt to obtain', and removing 'exemplary' limits the remedies available to the disclosing party in case of a breach, potentially weakening trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While it doesn't explicitly define 'irreparable injury,' case law interpreting this statute consistently recognizes the potential for irreparable harm in trade secret cases and allows for injunctive relief. Weakening the language around remedies may make it harder to obtain necessary relief.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1836 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1836 : Civil proceedings Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1836. Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract language states the disclosing party 'will suffer irreparable injury,' whereas the changed language states that the disclosing party 'may suffer injury.' The original language also states the disclosing party 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief' while the changed language states they 'shall be entitled to attempt to obtain injunctive relief.' Furthermore, the removal of 'exemplary' damages limits the remedies. This change weakens the protections afforded to trade secrets by making it less certain that the disclosing party will suffer irreparable harm and that they will be able to obtain injunctive relief. According to the Law Explanation, case law interpreting 18 U.S.C. § 1836 recognizes the potential for irreparable harm in trade secret cases and allows for injunctive relief. The change, therefore, could make it harder to obtain necessary relief. 18 USC 1836 (b)(1) states that 'An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection.' While this snippet itself doesn't directly address irreparable harm or injunctive relief, it does establish the right to bring a civil action for trade secret misappropriation. The altered language weakens the position of the disclosing party in such a civil action, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties and in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party or to enter into any other agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains some of the agreement between the Parties and in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party or to enter into any other agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'entire agreement' to 'some of the agreement' undermines the enforceability of the clause, creating an ambiguity that contradicts the intent of having a clear contract that includes the entire understanding. This makes it easier for parties to argue that other agreements exist outside of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-202, the Parol Evidence Rule, generally prevents parties from introducing extrinsic evidence (prior agreements) that contradicts a fully integrated contract. An integration clause stating that the agreement is entire and complete is evidence of full integration. By weakening the integration clause, the effect of the Parol Evidence Rule is lessened, allowing a party to bring in previous communications and understandings during legal proceedings and contradict this written agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement contains the 'entire agreement,' while the changed text says it contains 'some of the agreement.' UCC § 2-202, as per Scraped Law Snippet 1, prevents parties from introducing extrinsic evidence that contradicts a final written agreement. By changing 'entire' to 'some,' the contract suggests that other agreements might exist outside the written document. This directly undermines the parol evidence rule, as it opens the door for parties to argue that unwritten or prior agreements should also be considered part of the overall agreement. The change weakens the integration clause and allows parties to introduce prior agreements, contradicting the purpose of UCC § 2-202 which aims for finality in written agreements.
